attributes.stem: A 48-year-old man presents with a three month history of frequent episodes of faintness and light-headedness. These symptoms are worse shortly after a meal and may last about an hour. During this time he also experiences abdominal bloating and discomfort. His medical history includes hypertension and obesity. He takes perindopril and underwent a Roux-en-Y gastric bypass for management of his morbid obesity four months ago. On examination his blood pressure is 130/90 mmHg, pulse 90/min and temperature 37.0˚C. His physical examination is unremarkable apart from a BMI of 33.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This man is experiencing the dumping syndrome. This term is used to describe the symptoms that may occur in patients who have rapid and uncontrolled gastric emptying. There are a number of different causes for dumping and the term was first introduced to describe the vasomotor and gastrointestinal symptoms that occurred when the stomach was observed to empty rapidly after various forms of gastric surgery. These included procedures done for peptic ulcer disease (gastric resection, gastroenterostomy and vagotomy) and gastric cancer. Whilst surgery no longer has a key role in the management of peptic ulcer disease, gastric compartmentalisation and resection are now well established procedures in the management of obesity. Some 50% of patients who undergo a gastric bypass may experience dumping.

There are two components to dumping - an early phase and a late phase. The early phase occurs about 15 minutes after a meal and is comprised of vasomotor symptoms - dizziness, light-headedness and nausea. These symptoms are consequent on the relative hypovolaemia that may occur as fluid shifts from the circulating volume into the small bowel in response to the high osmotic load of food materials suddenly arriving in the small bowel. The late phase of dumping occurs an hour or after a meal and is due to a reactive hypoglycaemia (an overcompensation) from sudden release of insulin to handle the high carbohydrate suddenly entering the small bowel. Overall, after gastric bypass surgery patients are more likely to experience early rather than late dumping.

All patients who have had any form of gastric resection should be counselled on the need to eat small meals, with a moderate carbohydrate intake. In other words, adherence to a proper diet will usually alleviate their symptoms. The type of diet recommended is small, divided meals with no liquids 30 minutes after meals along with avoidance of carbohydrates. There should also be an increase in fibre to increase transit time.

Medications are an alternative with some like octreotide, propranolol or acarbose which all aim to either decrease transition time, vasoconstrict, decrease sugar breakdown or other methods that reduces dumping syndrome. These drugs, together with others such as metoclopramide and ondansetron, have shown efficacy in such reduction. However, these options are not as effective as dietary modification.

Octreotide can inhibit a number of gastrointestinal hormones with subsequent delay in gastric emptying, prolonged small intestine transit time, decreased insulin release and splanchnic vasoconstriction. Various studies have shown that octreotide can alleviate symptoms of dumping syndrome both as short and long-term therapy. However, this agent should only be tried if diet modifications fail to relieve the dumping symptoms.

Acarbose is a competitive inhibitor of α-glucosidase and the breakdown of oligosaccharides to monosaccharides is slowed. This action helps prevent the untoward effects of late dumping. It has been shown in a number of studies that acarbose can lead to a reduction in symptoms of postprandial hypoglycaemia.

Surgery would be the last line management approach to dumping and as judged from revisional procedures done for peptic ulcer disease, will not necessarily produce any better outcome.
learningPoints: The dumping syndrome is a relatively common consequence of gastric bypass surgery and can usually be managed by dietary advice.
references: Berg P, McCallum R. Dumping Syndrome: A Review of the Current Concepts of Pathophysiology, Diagnosis, and Treatment. Dig Dis Sci 2016; 61:11-18.

Hui C, Dhakal A, Bauza GJ. Dumping Syndrome. [Updated 2022 Jun 27]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470542/

Ellsmere JC. Late complications of bariatric surgical operations [Internet]. UpToDate. 2021 [cited 2022Nov8].
